“Yeah, for sure,” Kalkbrenner said. “We’re a young team. You don’t have it all figured out right away. Taking every opportunity we can to grow, have those character wins, is big for us. Every time you take a step further, it’s good. Just got to keep building.”

That’s essentially the thesis of this Hornets season in a handful of words. They’re not a finished product, and that should be obvious. But they’re also not waiting to become one before they take the moment seriously. They’re building in real time, in real games, with real consequences.

]]><![CDATA[KINGS WEEKLY PODCAST]]>https://www.basketballintelligence.net/p/kings-weekly-podcast-b49https://www.basketballintelligence.net/p/kings-weekly-podcast-b49Thu, 02 Apr 2026 14:31:29 GMTNick and Ray start their three-part series on the Sacramento Kings rookie class with Nique Clifford, the 24th pick in the 2025 NBA Draft. They cover his two-way versatility and some of his areas for improvement. They talk about his shooting, his struggles to start the season, and his recent better shooting. They also talk about his advanced defense for a rookie and his outlook on that end going forward. They wrap up by picking a song title that describes what they realistically think the Kings chances are of becoming a serious contender sometime in the next 4 years.

Basketball Intelligence Newsletter is a reader-supported publication. To receive every new post please become a paid subscriber.

“I think that’s telling that Steph would say that and there is an innate fear in people to fail in big spots or to be nervous. I think it’s just important to admit it at times and then kind of move on from there,” Roberts said this spring. “We were talking about it a couple days ago, those moments I think you mitigate the fear or the nerves from preparedness. I think Steph has shot so many free throws when he’s tired in big spots, so to admit that he’s nervous, he knows at the end of the day that he’s prepared for that spot. Most of our guys, we talk about that a lot, so I think that’s how they can get past that fear or nerve hurdle.”

Ray’s personal note: When I was a volunteer assistant at New Haven’s Wilbur Cross h.s. in 1968, we won the Connecticut state title with a 24-1 record. Our only loss was to Bob’s Hartford Public team due to his spectacular 28 point, 25 rebound performance. We split the regular season series and defeated HPHS in the state tournament. How good was our team that Bob showed out against? Legendary WCHS Head Coach Bob Saulsbury refers to it as the best of his 9 state champions. Our roster included 2 seniors who were first-team all-New England players (Alex Scott & Clint Davis) as well as emerging superstar John Williamson, who was a sophomore and already showing the talent that would take him to ABA & NBA stardom.

George Karl: These are the 3 best leaders I coached in the NBA

The second concept goes even further, a 22-team lottery that brings in the four teams eliminated in the first round of the playoffs. It also uses a two-year record component and a minimum win threshold concept, like 25 wins, to discourage extreme tanks.

This is the one that makes the biggest chunk of people uneasy because it introduces the most subjectivity and the most edge cases. A two-year formula can punish teams that crater for reasons that have nothing to do with intentional losing, and a win floor can feel like the league retroactively assigning results to smooth out the standings

His greatest contributions have come as a connector, ranking in the 88th percentile by creating 1.04 points per possession as a pick-and-roll ball handler despite shooting 40.4% from the field himself. His isolation efficiency dropped to the 6th percentile of the league with 0.57 PPP. Tatum mentioned not feeling fully confident in his burst until the loss to the Spurs, where he drove past Victor Wembanyama after a jab-step. He’s received fewer opportunities to create, ranking fourth on the team with 65.8 touches per game (2.4 seconds on average), compared to his team-leading 84.9 (3.7 sec. avg.) last year.

PHI: VJ Edgecombe and Justin Edwards have career nights in Sixers’ win at the Kings
Gabriella Carroll, The Inquirer

A big reason that group was especially effective on Thursday was its three-point shooting. As a trio, they made 13 threes, the lion’s share of the Sixers’ 17 made threes on 47.2% shooting from deep. Edwards hit a career-high seven three-pointers on 11 attempts. With every game, Edwards makes the case for why he should stay in the healthy rotation on a team low on effective three-point shooters.

In the previous 11 games, the Sixers shot just 27% from beyond the arc, which ranked last in the NBA. The last time the Sixers made 17 three-pointers was against Miami on Feb. 26, with both Maxey and Embiid still in the lineup.

Some of the help Thursday came from unexpected places. Before the 2025-26 season, Andre Drummond had made 23 total three-pointers in his 13 NBA seasons. In 2025-26, Drummond has made 26 three-pointers, including knocking down three corner threes against Sacramento on Thursday.

BI Note: Once again, the question re Kings’ horrific 3-point defense rears its ugly head: Scheme, personnel or effort?
